w:r.w:t: CHAPTER 1: STARDUST TO STARDUST
You cannot save someone who refuses saving.

I offer you this at the outset because the story will ask you to test it more than once, and I would rather you had the weight of it in your hands before we begin. I have watched grief work on people since before the concept had a name — grief, that is, not people, people came later — and it has never once behaved the way the person experiencing it believed it would. It is not a wound that heals. It is a direction a person chooses and then, having chosen, insists they did not choose.
Aviel chose it. This is the first fact. Everything else proceeds from here.

A four-room house in Hebron had become the shape of one man's will divided against itself — not metaphor but architecture: the western chamber sealed against the eastern, the northern passage choked with seven years of accumulated refusal, each room a record of what remains when grief mistakes itself for patience and patience mistakes itself for virtue. You might ask how a dwelling comes to resemble something as treacherous as a divided will. Such a question, posed while reading this, means you and I will get along.

I was there, you understand. I have been there. I have sat in the particular corner of that particular room where the torchlight does not quite reach, and I have watched this family with the attention of a watcher who cannot be deceived about what is actually happening — a watcher who has, it must be said, a tail to tell that is considerably longer than the threads in a ball of yarn, but that is a story for another chapter entirely. What was actually happening had been compounding, the way a debt compounds: invisibly at first, then with the specific and terrible efficiency of something that has been waiting for precisely the right moment to show you the total.

Father Aviel had been the cleverest scholar in Hebron — sharp as fresh-knapped flint, sought and summoned by those whose questions twisted beyond their own unraveling. Then Zuna passed. Nameless there forevermore. And something in him stopped. Not died — death would have been cleaner, quicker, and considerably less interesting to observe — it simply stopped, the way a cart stops when its axle shatters. The part of him that moved forward sat down in the dust and waited for nothing in particular. The gods he had gathered from the western plains — brittle bronze forms of forgotten flame, of fading fortune, of fertility that promised and did not deliver — found themselves swept away. All except one. The lord of inexhaustible dust and earthly plenty stayed, whispering that hoarded objects might hold back time himself, just to spite whatever kept making new things. That one stayed. That one, I warn you now, was paying considerably more attention than anyone in that house knew.

She had been remarkable, the mother. She sang while she worked her loom, and her voice moved through stone the way honey moves through water — slowly, completely, leaving the water changed. The threads she wove ran crimson like pomegranate seeds split open, saffron like desert flowers at dawn, emerald like new wheat through dark soil, amber like honey held to light. Every color under heaven. Never blue. Never purple. Never scarlet. She never explained why, and the boy never asked, and now neither of them could. Before she died she pressed a small river stone into his palm — smooth as a finished thought, carved with her own hand. It is from the stars, Danny, she said. All things are. We are stardust that forgot it was stardust, that got so caught up in being mud it forgot where it came from. So when the world feels heavy — and it will, it always does — remember: your home is not here. It is up there, in the cold clean fire of heaven where things burn without being consumed. He had worn it at his chest every day since. The stone was cold. It was meant to be cold. Cold is what the truth feels like when it has decided not to flatter you.

Now — the boy himself. Sixteen. Kind eyes that had seen too much sorrow, the specific kind of kindness that sorrow produces in people who choose to keep it rather than spend it on bitterness. Unruly black hair he kept pressing flat with one hand, which continued to refuse his instruction. He started at the hearth: crouching low, striking flint against pyrite until the sparks scattered in a brief gold cascade and the pitch-soaked rag caught, guttering once before committing. The frail flame trembled now not from wind but from the staccato percussion against his ribs — the body's oldest instrument, the one that plays regardless of the musician's preferences. Each barefoot step through the corridor was a wager against sound. One shard of pottery breaking beneath his weight, and the house would know.

The corridor choked with things that had long ceased to be merely things. Broken pottery lay scattered like shards of failed offerings — Dan had counted eighteen visible pieces one morning and then stopped, unable to push past that number. Eighteen. The ancient tongue uses it to spell the word for life. Boundary between what still counted as living and what had crossed over. He simply stopped there each time, as if the number itself had mass. Fragments of parchment and brittle wax tablets lay stripped clean by mold that had developed, over seven years, what could only be called genuine ambition — spreading in patterns too deliberate for accident, following lines no craftsman had drawn. Ruined brass astrolabes gathered in the shadowed corners, their rings seized into arrangements pointing with insistent consistency toward a direction corresponding to no coordinate in heaven or on earth. His mother's loom stood against one wall. The threads had rotted to mildew, but you could still see what they had been.

From the darkest pocket of the corridor — the corner the torchlight did not quite reach — something made no sound. Not in the manner of ordinary silence. The distinction was slight. Dan noticed it the way you notice a room has someone in it before you locate the someone.

The smell was architecture. Designed, one might almost say, by someone who hated the very idea of breath. Ammonia sharp enough to raise involuntary water to the eyes. Decay sweet as overripe fruit three days past its reckoning. Maggots worked the rotted cloth with what looked disturbingly like enthusiasm — fat things the size of a thumb, as purposeful as priests at a rite no one had thought to cancel. Flies rose and fell in low patient clouds. Their droning was this house's liturgy, as established and particular as any psalm. Beneath everything, the mineral reek of disturbed earth, of things buried being dragged back into light. The house wanted to drag them both down into its rot with the infinite patience of decay — the specific patience of something that does not need to hurry because it already owns the destination. Dan had stopped registering any of this six years ago. The fact that it returned to him tonight told him something about what he was carrying into his father's room.

Some few children come into the world able to remain awake inside the country where all dreamers travel. Not awake as the body is awake — no muscle moves, no eyelid shifts — but present, with the complete attention of a watcher who cannot be deceived. Dan's mother had been one of these. She had begun teaching him when he was still young enough to learn without understanding what was being handed to him. To enter another dreamer's country required proximity — the closer the watcher, the cleaner the passage — and this was why Dan moved toward his father's door rather than attempting the work from his own mat. The method requires more discipline than most adults possess, which is perhaps why most adults are not dreamwalkers. Release yesterday, letting it dissolve as salt dissolves in deep water. Release tomorrow, unhooking from its obligations. Then release now — the difficult one — until the body achieves the stillness of deep water and what is true may surface in it. He had practiced this for five years. Tonight it had a destination.

Dan arrived at his father's door and went still — not the patience of serpents in a garden, but the particular stillness of someone listening through stone for a sound they need to hear. From the other side came the sound of a man trying to outrun his own breath: ragged, straining, the body running drills for a crisis it could not locate. Dan waited. The breath gradually deepened, steadied, lost its urgency. Then, finally, the slow heavy pull of a man gone fully under.
He extinguished the torch against the wall.

The dark arrived with the confidence of something that has been here many times before. Dan pressed his palm flat to the doorframe — cold, slightly rough, exactly as solid as stone had always been regardless of what was asked of it — and said, barely above a breath, to himself, the doorframe simply being the nearest object: Up. Even if it kills me. Even if I am ground to dust on the way. Up. Then he closed his eyes.

Released yesterday. Released tomorrow. Released the corridor, the smell, the percussion, the cold coming up through his soles. One loop at a time from the post. Until the body became still water. Until the dark behind his lids changed quality — from the dark of a closed room to the dark of something vast and formless and older than the thing that named it.
It was formless void, and darkness upon the face of the deep.
Then, the way a door made of deep water opens, he was through.

The dreamscape received him. The formless dark churned below and the void was total above — as things must have been before the first word of the first morning was spoken into the dark that had not yet known it was waiting. Dan oriented quickly; he knew this country the way a man knows the house he grew up in. He turned toward his father and found the barrier immediately: a wall thick and pulsing with old bruise-light, sick and humid, like a bruise that had achieved consciousness and learned to hate everything that was not pain.

Something bled through it that was not Dan's. A child's scream cut short. Blood on white stone. A voice calling one name and stopping mid-syllable. A city's final scream. The bone-deep shame of a man who cannot locate the moment he should have acted differently. For one moment the thought surfaced: some barriers are meant to stand. Then Dan pressed his whole intention against it.

The wall broke — total and sudden as a held breath released — and he stumbled through into a wasteland beneath an ashen sky. Father Aviel stood at the plain's center with his arms wrapped around himself as if to keep his shape, waiting in the posture of a man who has been waiting so long he has forgotten what he waits for.

Dan lifted his palm toward the emptiness above. Intent was enough. Rocks descended from the motionless sky — not clean rain, but hard mineral chunks, angular and indifferent. Aviel looked up. One struck his shoulder. He picked it up and in the peculiar logic of the place it transformed — became gold, dense and cold, carrying the specific weight of what men will abandon their families for. His eyes went wide. More fell, each transforming: copper pieces, silver rings, gems holding impossible colors — rubies like frozen blood, sapphires like captured sky, emeralds like distilled envy. Aviel gathered with the frantic efficiency of a man receiving exactly the permission he has waited his entire life for. Mine, he whispered — half prayer, half curse — trying to fill a hole that went straight through to the other side of the world. The pile rose past his ankles, his knees, his waist. Past his chest. Past his neck. Aviel was buried to the chin, his hands frozen mid-grasp, fingers fused to the metal, his face cycling through dark red and then the deep purple of something being pressed closed from outside.

Dan raised his palm a final time. The wind that came was cold and total, gathering everything into a spiral, scattering it all back into dust, and the dust dissolved as it fell. The plain emptied. Aviel knelt alone with his hands still shaped for holding. For one moment his eyes cleared. He looked up, and above him appeared a single star — small and cold as the truest things are. He stared at it with the expression of a man discovering he has been forgiven for something he never confessed. Something in him cracked — not broke entirely, but cracked enough to admit one thin shaft of light. The star faded. The plain dissolved. Aviel drifted down toward dreamless sleep.

Dan gasped back into the corridor. Stone against his knees. The smell, a solid wall. Torch out. He moved by feel until the warmth of the banked coals met his face before his eyes found the glow. Touched pitch to ember. The torch caught. He stood with the returned light and said, toward the flame, in the voice a sixteen-year-old uses when speaking to something that is not listening but might be: Harder than I thought. Then waited. Then the smaller thing after the silence. He had been taught to say all three parts even when only the silence came back.

Now — and I say this as one who made a very particular study of this boy from the preferred corners of this house, for longer than I will presently confess — there is nothing remarkable about a clean room. People have them everywhere. What is remarkable is a clean room maintained by deliberate act inside a house that is actively trying to become something else. While the western passage filled with crates and the corridor accumulated its purposeful mold, Dan's room had been kept with a precision that spoke not of tidiness but of decision. The blanket folded with crisp corners. The floor swept clean. His possessions arranged with careful economy: the clay cup, the wooden comb his mother had carved from a fig branch, the flint stylus for marking stone, one small window kept clear so that morning light could enter — which it did, daily, whether or not anyone stood in it to receive it. He had maintained this since the age of ten. Without instruction. Without acknowledgment. Without expectation of reward. One might say — and one does say, because one was there — that Dan had put his own house in order before daring to concern himself with the disorder outside his door. The room was the hard-won proof of it. He would not have used those words. The room was there regardless.

His shadow, thrown by the flame against the wall behind him, was hunched and wrong — the silhouette of a much older exhaustion compressed into a sixteen-year-old frame. He did not see it. He lowered himself onto the cot, pressed the torch into its crack in the wall, and lay back. Barely shaped into words, he said something to the ceiling stone that ended with Mother and contained the word stardust. The ceiling stone offered nothing. He had not asked it to. The exhaustion took him without announcement, the way a tide takes what the shore has been holding.
What came was not rest. It was the sleep the body seizes when the body has stopped asking the will for permission.

Dan found himself standing on water — bare feet on a surface that was liquid at one angle and solid at another, the way certain truths hold only when approached directly. The sky above held a blue found only in the country where waking and sleeping share a border. He took one step. The water held. In the distance sat a boat, but Dan dispersed it with a thought — in this place you keep only what you need. The water thickened beneath him, went clear, became something harder — cold as polished obsidian, black at depth. He could see down into distances with no floor. Shapes moved below with the unhurried deliberation of things that have all of time.

Then he heard it: a single crystalline snap. Beautiful, in a terrible way — the fracture lines racing out with the speed of certainty, the sound building from whisper to roar in three slow breaths. Then the floor of the world exploded upward.

The wave hit him like a wall collapsing. He went under at once, the cold driving through every layer of him. When he surfaced the sky had gone the color of a wound — deep purple bleeding to obsidian, clouds assembling into shapes that moved with intent. The sea churned white at the crests and black in the troughs. The foam at his lips tasted of brine and something beneath brine — mineral, ancient, absolute. Below, the light in the water changed. Something was ascending from directly underneath him, and the sea above it went white from the force of the pressure. The water shoved Dan back. The waves became mountains.

The dream began its diminuendo — the phantom opera of the deep descending through its lowest registers — and then, from the southern edge of the world where the horizon bled into endless turbulent dark, it rose.
Not all at once. This is how it comes.

First: a forehead. Immense, streaming, the color of drowned stone. Bearing the marks of eons of pressure — not damage, but record. Then the eyes: wide as abysses, blacker than the deepest trench, without reflection, without the movement that living eyes carry — eyes that had been open in the dark longer than memory. Then the rest assembling below: barnacled cheeks, the jaw broad as a city gate, seaweed draped at the chin like the vestments of something that had its own priests before the first priest drew breath. No expression moved in that face. That was the detail that made the waves seem ordinary. It regarded him with the completeness of stone regarding weather. And far to the south — too far to see but known with dream-certainty — a massive tail broke the surface, spanning horizon to horizon.
The voice arrived not from the mouth but from inside the architecture of Dan's skull. A resonant hum that shook the roots of his teeth.
"I am Dagon, the power thy father hath chosen. Lord of Abundance Manifest, Master of Accumulation Eternal. Ask and receive, for my path is ease."
"I didn't call you!"

"Thou hast ever called. Thy very flesh doth so, seeking its appointed rest." The eyes did not blink. "But this night hast thou sinned most grievously, daring to undo my work established within thy father's dwelling through seven years of deliberate cultivation. Do not mistake thy small, transient motion for a living offering worthy of divine consideration."
"Abundance of things? Why devote my life to what I can't carry through death?"

"I offer unto thee a raft most substantial, so that the friction of death shall lose its sting. Come, follow me into the comfortable depths. Thy mother's voice I shall restore to thee in nocturnal visions — dreams wherein she speaketh comfort unceasing. I shall bestow upon thee a wife whose hands need not be reddened by caustic lye, and sons who shall never comprehend their grandfather's shame. I offer the sluggish, sweet warmth of a life forgotten entirely by the cruelties which the heights inflict without mercy."

No — not a vision. A physical press through the long bones, through the marrow, before any other part of him could refuse it. A house with lamps lit. A woman at a low table. Laughter from past an open door. The pull registered the way a deep well registers in a man standing too close to the edge — the body's own weight understood suddenly as the beginning of a fall. Something in Dan answered it before his direction could intervene.
His hand found the stone at his chest. Cold. Sharp-edged against his ribs. Cold the way the truth is cold: not cruel, just indifferent to comfort.
"Your temple fell to a blinded man. You crumpled before the Sacred Chest."

Something moved through the vast face — not expression. A reorganization. "My temple fell not for want of My weakness, boy. My statue crumbled not for absence of My power. There exist boundaries ancient which must not be transgressed; the realm of the living cannot trespass into the realm of the dead without consequence. We are bound by different laws immutable. Mistake not My ancient restraint — discipline practiced across eons — for weakness."
"So you admit it. You are a Being of Death. A gatekeeper to the pit."

"Look about thee, boy." The eyes lowered — not to Dan, to the sea, to the shapes moving far below in the dark. "Look at the war within thee. Look at the wars between men. Death is not a distant shore. It is the very sea wherein thou dost stand."
"Why should I serve you and not what holds higher?"

"The one who pursueth the heights becometh the monument isolated — cold, absolute, and alone." Something entered the vast eyes that was not anger and was worse than anger. "Choose the heights if thou must. But know this: the silence of the cosmos awaits thee. The star thou becomest shall burn alone. When thou speakest, no man shall comprehend. Thy instrument shall be shattered beyond repair. Thy tongue shall be made a heavy thing, speaking foretold truth that is heard only as the worthless babble of the judged, like chaff that the wind drives away." The pause that followed had the weight of a verdict already handed down and only now delivered. "Thou wilt have no witness. Knock thrice, ask twice, then wait. I am the cadence thou seekest to outrun. Thou only choosest when we play."
Dan got a full mouthful of the sea and let it run back out. "I watched what your comfort bought my father. I'd drink this sea before I'd live one more day in your filth."

Dagon laughed — the low foundational sound of an ocean deciding on a new shore: patient, geological, certain of its arrival. "A brave little mouse. Thou choosest the high places? Very well. I am the very earth those mountains are rooted within; thy struggle ascending is my foundation sustaining." The phantom opera of the deep reached its final measure. "If ever thou shouldst alter thy tone — merely knock thrice, ask twice, then wait. I shall come, for I am the cadence thou seekest to outrun."
The black water filled Dan's mouth, his nose, his lungs. Everything went dark.

Dan woke with salt in his throat — not dream-salt only but sweat, the body's own brine, the frantic furnace within announcing itself against his sternum. He ran the inventory: the clean-swept floor, the possessions in their order, the torch burning low in its crack, the window showing the sky at its earliest possible stage of deciding whether to become morning. Everything felt drained — the familiar objects remote and mute, the world's colors diluted one shade toward ash, as if the dreamwalk had taken something and left the shells of things behind.

He rose and went to the window. Hebron's rooftops lay below in a darkness barely beginning to reconsider itself. He pressed his palm to the stone sill — cold working into his palm slowly, thoroughly — and said, toward the sill, to himself: Stardust to stardust. His voice came out smaller than intended. Mother, tell me it means something. Tell me I didn't waste it.
The sky above Hebron did not answer. He had not expected it to.

He returned to the cot. The torch in its crack burned lower still, the flame reduced to a coal-colored glow, coming to its decision. He pulled the thin blanket to his chin. The flatness that settled into him was not peace and not rest but the total evacuation of further capacity. Like chaff that the wind drives away, he thought — not certain whether the thought was resignation or quotation or both. His eyes closed.

What came was not restful. No more than the sleep his father was sleeping behind the debris-choked wall across the corridor was restful. They were sleeping the same sleep now, father and son: the sleep of men who have each spent the night on something that mattered, and do not yet know if it mattered enough.

Two things arrived at once. Not one. Two. Both patient. Pointing in opposite directions. The house is divided, he thought, from somewhere in the country between sleep and waking. The house has always been divided.

From below — beneath the foundation stone, beneath the packed soil, from wherever the roots of things descend past any lamp's reach — a grinding. Slow and dense and absolute: the specific sound of something that knows exactly where it is and has no urgency whatsoever about what comes next. It did not knock. Did not call. Simply became more present, the way a shape assembles in a dark room when the eye finally stops avoiding it. It carried in it, pressed like a seal into wet clay, the impression of a phrase not yet finished with him.

And at the door of the clean and ordered room — at the threshold he had maintained for six years against the encroachment of everything outside it — something altogether different. Not quite rapping, not quite tapping: the patient investigation of something curious enough to inquire and wise enough to wait. A late visitor entreating entrance at his chamber lattice. Curious as well as patient. Warm in a way the grinding below was not — warm as a coal kept burning in an empty room on the chance that someone might eventually return.
Two presences. One below the floor. One at the door.
The torch gave its last.

In the fraction of stillness between flame-end and first starlight through the window — in that interval belonging to neither fire nor sky — the house pressed upward through its foundation the only verdict it knew how to render.
"No witness," echoed as the last light failed.
